voventa's blog

Codeforces Round 1032 (Div. 3) Editorial
By voventa, history, 9 months ago, translation,

Thank you all for participating! 👊👊👊

2121A - Letter Home

Tutorial

2121A - Letter Home

Notice that if we visit positions x1x1 and xnxn, we will necessarily visit all other positions xixi. Therefore, our goal will be to visit positions x1x1 and xnxn. We then have two options:

  • From position ss, go to position x1x1, and from there go to position xnxn. We will need to make |s−x1|+|xn−x1||s−x1|+|xn−x1| steps.
  • From position ss, go to position xnxn, and from there go to position x1x1. We will need to make |s−xn|+|xn−x1||s−xn|+|xn−x1| steps.

From the two cases, we choose the smaller one. The answer will be min(|s−x1|,|s−xn|)+xn−x1min(|s−x1|,|s−xn|)+xn−x1. The solution can be implemented in O(n)O(n).

Code
#include<bits/stdc++.h>

using namespace std;

int main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0);
    cin.tie(0);
    cout.tie(0);
    int t;
    cin >> t;
    while (t--) {
        int n, s;
        cin >> n >> s;
        vector<int> x(n);
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) cin >> x[i];
        int ans = min(abs(s - x[0]), abs(s - x.back())) + x.back() - x[0];
        cout << ans << '\n';
    }
    return 0;
}

2121B - Above the Clouds

Tutorial

2121B - Above the Clouds

Notice that if there exist three non-empty strings aa, bb, and cc that satisfy the condition, then there exist three non-empty strings a′a′, b′b′, and c′c′ that satisfy the condition, where the length of string b′b′ is equal to 11. To achieve this, one can choose any character from string bb, append all characters before it to the end of string aa, and prepend all characters after it to the beginning of string cc.

Let cntlcntl be the number of times character ll appears in string ss. Then the answer "Yes" will be given if any of the following conditions hold:

  • There exists a character ll such that cntl≥3cntl≥3. We can choose the second occurrence of character ll in string ss as string bb.
  • There exists a character ll such that cntl=2cntl=2 and either the first or the last character of string ss is not equal to ll. We can choose any occurrence of character ll in the string as string bb, except when it is the first or last character of string ss.

The solution can be implemented in O(n)O(n).

Code
#include<bits/stdc++.h>

using namespace std;

int main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0);
    cin.tie(0);
    cout.tie(0);
    int t;
    cin >> t;
    while (t--) {
        int n;
        cin >> n;
        string s;
        cin >> s;
        vector<int> cnt(26, 0);
        for (auto c : s) cnt[c - 'a']++;
        int flag = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < 26; i++) {
            if (cnt[i] >= 3) flag = 1;
            else if (cnt[i] == 2 && (s[0] - 'a' != i || s.back() - 'a' != i)) flag = 1;
        }
        if (flag) cout << "Yes" << '\n';
        else cout << "No" << '\n';
    }
    return 0;
}

2121C - Those Who Are With Us

Tutorial

2121C - Those Who Are With Us

Let mxmx be the maximum value in the matrix. Note that the answer to the problem will be either mx−1mx−1 or mxmx.

When will the answer be mx−1mx−1? If there exists a pair (r,c)(r,c) such that all values of mxmx are contained in row rr or column cc.

Let rowrrowr be the number of times mxmx appears in row rr; colccolc be the number of times mxmx appears in column cc. Then in row rr or column cc, the number mxmx appears rowr+colcrowr+colc times. However, there is an edge case: if arc=mxarc=mx, we have counted it twice, so we need to subtract one. If this count equals the total occurrences of mxmx in the matrix, then we can achieve the answer mx−1mx−1.

The solution can be implemented in O(n⋅m)O(n⋅m).

Code
#include<bits/stdc++.h>

using namespace std;

main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0);
    cin.tie(0);
    cout.tie(0);
    int t;
    cin >> t;
    while (t--) {
        int n, m;
        cin >> n >> m;
        vector<vector<int>> a(n, vector<int> (m));
        int mx = 0, cnt_mx = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            for (int j = 0; j < m; j++) {
                cin >> a[i][j];
                if (a[i][j] > mx) {
                    mx = a[i][j], cnt_mx = 1;
                } else if (a[i][j] == mx) {
                    cnt_mx++;
                }
            }
        }
        vector<int> r(n), c(m);
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            for (int j = 0; j < m; j++) {
                if (a[i][j] == mx) {
                    r[i]++;
                    c[j]++;
                }
            }
        }
        int flag = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            for (int j = 0; j < m; j++) {
                if (r[i] + c[j] - (a[i][j] == mx) == cnt_mx) {
                    flag = 1;
                }
            }
        }
        cout << mx - flag << '\n';
    }
    return 0;
}

2121D - 1709

Tutorial

2121D - 1709

Using bubble sort, we will sort array aa: while there exists an index 1≤i<n1≤i<n such that ai>ai+1ai>ai+1, we will swap them. Similarly, we will sort array bb. In this part of the task, we will perform no more than n⋅(n−1)2+n⋅(n−1)2=n⋅(n−1)n⋅(n−1)2+n⋅(n−1)2=n⋅(n−1) operations, since the number of swaps in bubble sort is equal to the number of inversions in the array.

Next, for all 1≤i≤n1≤i≤n such that ai>biai>bi, we will swap aiai and bibi. After this, we will satisfy both conditions and perform a total of no more than n⋅(n−1)+n=n2n⋅(n−1)+n=n2 operations.

The solution can be implemented in O(n2)O(n2).

Code
#include<bits/stdc++.h>

using namespace std;

main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0);
    cin.tie(0);
    cout.tie(0);
    int t;
    cin >> t;
    while (t--) {
        int n;
        cin >> n;
        vector<int> a(n), b(n);
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) cin >> a[i];
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) cin >> b[i];
        vector<pair<int, int>> ans;
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            for (int j = 1; j < n; j++) {
                if (a[j - 1] > a[j]) {
                    swap(a[j - 1], a[j]);
                    ans.push_back({1, j});
                }
            }
        }
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            for (int j = 1; j < n; j++) {
                if (b[j - 1] > b[j]) {
                    swap(b[j - 1], b[j]);
                    ans.push_back({2, j});
                }
            }
        }
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            if (a[i] > b[i]) {
                ans.push_back({3, i + 1});
            }
        }
        cout << ans.size() << '\n';
        for (auto [x, y] : ans) cout << x << " " << y << '\n';
    }
    return 0;
}

2121E - Sponsor of Your Problems

Tutorial

2121E - Sponsor of Your Problems

Note that the number xx will have the same length in decimal representation as the numbers ll and rr.

Consider the example l=12345l=12345 and r=12534r=12534. For the number xx to be in the range between ll and rr inclusive, it must be of length 55 and start with the digits 1212. In other words, the greatest common prefix of the numbers ll and rr will be the beginning of the number xx.

Next, look at the digits following the greatest common prefix, which in this case are 33 and 55. If these digits differ by at least two, we can choose a digit between them, excluding the boundaries, and then fill in the remaining lower digits such that there are no common digits with the numbers ll and rr. In this case, for example, we can take the number x=12400x=12400: we chose the digit 44 between 33 and 55, and then we can choose the lower two digits arbitrarily—let's choose them so that there are no common digits with the numbers ll and rr. In summary, if the digits after the greatest common prefix differ by at least two, the answer will be equal to twice the common prefix.

Now consider the example l=1239990l=1239990 and r=1240037r=1240037. Again, we can conclude that the number xx will be of length 77 and start with the digits 1212. But in this case, the digits after the greatest common prefix differ by 11, which means we need to choose one of them. Let's consider two options for selection:

  • The number xx will be of length 77 and start with the digits 123123. Then this number will definitely be <r<r. We just need to ensure that this number is not less than ll and minimize the number of common digits. Notice that the next digit in ll is 99, so we can guarantee that the number xx must start with the digits 123999123999.
  • The number xx will be of length 77 and start with the digits 124124. Then this number will definitely be >l>l. We just need to ensure that this number is not greater than rr and minimize the number of common digits. Notice that the next digit in rr is 00, so we can guarantee that the number xx must start with the digits 1240012400.

If we skip the complete breakdown of cases, the answer will consist of three parts that need to be summed:

  • Twice the common prefix.
  • One (since we are choosing one of the digits after the greatest common prefix, and it will be in one of the numbers).
  • The number of consecutive digits ii after the first differing digit in the numbers ll and rr, such that li=9li=9 and ri=0ri=0.

Thus, in this case, the answer is 2+1+2=52+1+2=5. We can choose the number x=1240001x=1240001, for example.

Code
#include<bits/stdc++.h>

using namespace std;

int main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0);
    cin.tie(0);
    cout.tie(0);
    int t;
    cin >> t;
    while (t--) {
        string l, r;
        cin >> l >> r;
        if (l == r) {
            cout << 2 * l.size() << '\n';
            continue;
        }
        int ptr = 0;
        while (ptr < l.size() && l[ptr] == r[ptr]) ptr++;
        if (l[ptr] + 1 < r[ptr]) {
            cout << 2 * ptr << '\n';
        } else {
            int res = 2 * ptr + 1;
            for (int i = ptr + 1; i < l.size(); i++) {
                if (l[i] == '9' && r[i] == '0') res++;
                else break;
            }
            cout << res << '\n';
        }
    }
    return 0;
}

2121F - Yamakasi

Tutorial

2121F - Yamakasi

To start, let's recall how to solve the following problem: how many subsegments of the array have a sum of elements equal to ss.

Let prefiprefi be the sum of the first ii elements of the array. Then the sum of a subsegment will be equal to prefr−prefl−1prefr−prefl−1. We will iterate over the right boundary rr and count the number of suitable left boundaries ll, that is, such boundaries that prefr−prefl−1=sprefr−prefl−1=s or prefl−1=prefr−sprefl−1=prefr−s. For this, we will additionally maintain a map to keep track of the number of left boundaries with a given prefix sum value for the considered prefix.

Now we must also take into account the fact that the maximum value must equal xx. For this, we will maintain a pointer leflef — the smallest left boundary that we have not yet added to the map, but can add when the maximum in the subsegment (lef,r)(lef,r) equals xx. Next, we have the following options:

  • If ar>xar>x, then we need to clear the map and set lef=r+1lef=r+1, since no good subsegment can contain the element arar.
  • If ar=xar=x, we need to add information about the left boundaries lef,lef+1,…,rlef,lef+1,…,r to the map (add information about their values prefl−1prefl−1). After this, set lef=r+1lef=r+1.
  • If ar<xar<x, nothing changes.

And similarly, for each right boundary, we can count the number of suitable left boundaries. The solution can be implemented in O(nlogn)O(nlog⁡n).

Code
#include<bits/stdc++.h>

using namespace std;

typedef long long ll;
int const maxn = 2e5 + 5;
int a[maxn];
ll pref[maxn], s;

main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0);
    cin.tie(0);
    cout.tie(0);
    int t;
    cin >> t;
    while (t--) {
        int n, x;
        cin >> n >> s >> x;
        for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
            cin >> a[i];
            pref[i] = pref[i - 1] + a[i];
        }
        ll ans = 0;
        map<ll, int> cnt;
        int lef = 1;
        for (int r = 1; r <= n; r++) {
            if (a[r] > x) cnt.clear(), lef = r + 1;
            else if (a[r] == x) {
                while (lef <= r) {
                    cnt[pref[lef - 1]]++;
                    lef++;
                }
            }
            ans += cnt[pref[r] - s];
        }
        cout << ans << '\n';
    }
    return 0;
}

2121G - Gangsta

Tutorial

2121G - Gangsta

Let c0c0 be the number of zeros in the subsegment, and c1c1 be the number of ones. Notice that max(c0,c1)=c0+c1+|c0−c1|2max(c0,c1)=c0+c1+|c0−c1|2. Then we will find the sum c0+c1+|c0−c1|c0+c1+|c0−c1| over all subsegments, and this sum divided by two will be the answer.

Let prefiprefi be the difference between the number of ones and the number of zeros in the prefix of length ii. Then f(slsl+1…sr)=r−l+1+|prefr−prefl−1|f(slsl+1…sr)=r−l+1+|prefr−prefl−1|. We will separately calculate the sum r−l+1r−l+1 over all subsegments. This can be done in O(n)O(n): the number of segments of length lenlen will be n−len+1n−len+1, so we need to find ∑len=1nlen⋅(n−len+1)∑len=1nlen⋅(n−len+1).

We still need to find the sum |prefr−prefl−1||prefr−prefl−1| over all subsegments. To do this, we will sort the array prefpref (here we need to be careful, as this array consists of n+1n+1 elements, not nn). After sorting, we will eliminate the absolute value, and we will need to find the sum prefr−prefl−1prefr−prefl−1 over all subsegments. This sum will be equal to ∑i=0nprefi⋅(i−(n−i))∑i=0nprefi⋅(i−(n−i)), since in ii terms we will take prefiprefi with a positive sign, and in n−in−i terms we will take it with a negative sign.

The solution can be implemented in O(nlogn)O(nlog⁡n).

Code
#include<bits/stdc++.h>

using namespace std;

typedef long long ll;
int const maxn = 2e5 + 5;
int pref[maxn];

main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0);
    cin.tie(0);
    cout.tie(0);
    int t;
    cin >> t;
    while (t--) {
        int n;
        cin >> n;
        string s;
        cin >> s;
        ll ans = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            pref[i + 1] = pref[i];
            if (s[i] == '0') pref[i + 1]--;
            else pref[i + 1]++;
        }
        for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
            ans += (ll)i * (n - i + 1);
        }
        sort(pref, pref + n + 1);
        for (int i = 0; i <= n; i++) {
            ans += (ll)pref[i] * (i - (n - i));
        }
        cout << ans / 2 << '\n';
    }
    return 0;
}

2121H - Ice Baby

Tutorial

2121H - Ice Baby

Let dp[i][j]dp[i][j] be the minimum number xx such that we can choose the values of the elements a1,a2,…,aia1,a2,…,ai in such a way that there exists a non-decreasing subsequence of length jj with the last element of the subsequence equal to xx. If it is impossible to choose a subsequence of length jj, we will consider dp[i][j]=infdp[i][j]=inf. We also note that dp[i][j]≤dp[i][j+1]dp[i][j]≤dp[i][j+1].

Consider the recalculation of the dynamics when transitioning from ii to i+1i+1:

  1. If dp[i][j]≤li+1dp[i][j]≤li+1, then dp[i+1][j]=dp[i][j]dp[i+1][j]=dp[i][j].
  2. If dp[i][j−1]≤ri+1dp[i][j−1]≤ri+1, then dp[i+1][j]=max(li+1,dp[i][j−1])dp[i+1][j]=max(li+1,dp[i][j−1]). It is worth noting that the number max(li+1,dp[i][j−1])max(li+1,dp[i][j−1]) will definitely not be greater than dp[i][j]dp[i][j] (if we have indeed entered this case and not the first one!).
  3. If dp[i][j−1]>ri+1dp[i][j−1]>ri+1, then dp[i+1][j]=dp[i][j]dp[i+1][j]=dp[i][j].

The first and third transitions are not of interest to us, as they preserve the previous value of the dynamics for a fixed jj. We only need to process the second transition. Here we will use the fact that dp[i][j]≤dp[i][j+1]dp[i][j]≤dp[i][j+1].

Let leflef be the smallest index such that dp[i][lef]≥lidp[i][lef]≥li; righrigh be the largest index such that dp[i][righ]≤ridp[i][righ]≤ri. The assertion is: then dp[i+1][lef]=ldp[i+1][lef]=l, dp[i+1][j]=dp[i][j−1]dp[i+1][j]=dp[i][j−1] for lef<j≤righ+1lef<j≤righ+1. Note that dpidpi is a multiset of values, and since dp[i][j]≤dp[i][j+1]dp[i][j]≤dp[i][j+1], the positions do not matter; only the values are important (they will uniquely determine the ii-th layer of dynamics). Let's see how the multiset of values dpidpi differs from dpi+1dpi+1:

  • An element ll is added.
  • At most one element is removed, namely the smallest element that is greater than rr (if such an element exists).

Therefore, it is sufficient to maintain only the multiset of values of the ii-th layer of dynamics (initially it is empty). We will handle two changes: insertion into the multiset (insert) and removal (erase) of the smallest element that is greater than a given number (such an element can be found using the upper_bound method).

The solution can be implemented in O(nlogn)O(nlog⁡n).

Code
#include<bits/stdc++.h>

using namespace std;

main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0);
    cin.tie(0);
    cout.tie(0);
    int t;
    cin >> t;
    while (t--) {
        int n;
        cin >> n;
        multiset<int> dp;
        for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
            int l, r;
            cin >> l >> r;
            auto it = dp.upper_bound(r);
            if (it != dp.end()) {
                dp.erase(it);
            }
            dp.insert(l);
            cout << dp.size() << " ";
        }
        cout << '\n';
    }
    return 0;
}

noobcoder180

noobcoder180
9 months ago, hide # |
← Rev. 2 →  
+26

For EE, I generated 100100 random numbers xx in the range [l,r][l,r] and took the minimum f(l,x)+f(x,r)f(l,x)+f(x,r) over all of them. I think that the worst-case probability that some xx works is (45)9(45)9 (or around 13%13%) because, in most cases, it seems like each digit of xx has a 4545 chance of not being equal to either of the corresponding digits of ll and rr. So the probability that the solution fails a testcase might be (1−(45)9)100≈5⋅10−7(1−(45)9)100≈5⋅10−7.

          noobcoder180
          9 months ago, hide # ^ |
          ← Rev. 2 →  
          +10

          Interesting. I might be wrong, but it looks like 300000000599999999300000000599999999 would be an optimal hacking testcase. That is because there is a 1313 chance of getting a 44 (as opposed to a 33 or a 55), and then a (45)8(45)8 chance of satisfying all of the other digits. So the chance of any one xx producing the answer is 13⋅(45)8≈5.5%13⋅(45)8≈5.5%. So the probability of failing this testcase is (1−13⋅(45)8)100≈0.3%(1−13⋅(45)8)100≈0.3%, which is really not good for 104104 testcases.

          And, unless there's a better testcase out there, I guess there's not a snowball's chance in hell you will be able to hack macaquedev, as (1−13⋅(45)8)1000≈10−25(1−13⋅(45)8)1000≈10−25.

          → Reply

      I mean exactly what I said. It's quite trivial to calculate score given l, r and some number x between l and r. You just implement what's written in the problem statement — compare each digit. Then, you generate 1000 random values of x, and with almost 100% certainty, at least one of them will have the minimal possible score. This is not hackable.

      → Reply

_Maddy1234
9 months ago, show (+9) #
»
_Maddy1234
9 months ago, hide # |
 
+3

in problem D why simply sorting a and b and then swaping is enough. can someone explain me clearly why swaping in last will not violate our conditions .?

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    reirugan
    9 months ago, show (+2) #
    »
    »
    reirugan
    9 months ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 2 →  
    +9

    Let aa and bb be the arrays after sorting, but before swapping.

    Then for any ii, we have that min(ai,bi)≤ai,bimin(ai,bi)≤ai,bi. But at least one of aiai or bibi must be smaller than min(ai+1,bi+1)min(ai+1,bi+1), because one of them was placed before min(ai+1,bi+1)min(ai+1,bi+1) whilst sorting. So we have that the mins are increasing.

    By similar reasoning, for any ii, we have that max(ai,bi)max(ai,bi) is less than at least one of ai+1ai+1 or bi+1bi+1 because max(ai,bi)max(ai,bi) was placed before one of them whilst sorting. Since we have that ai+1,bi+1≤max(ai+1,bi+1)ai+1,bi+1≤max(ai+1,bi+1), we have that the maxes are increasing.

    So we have that min(a1,b1)<min(a2,b2)<⋯<min(an,bn)min(a1,b1)<min(a2,b2)<⋯<min(an,bn) and max(a1,b1)<max(a2,b2)<⋯<max(an,bn)max(a1,b1)<max(a2,b2)<⋯<max(an,bn), so if we place all of the mins in aa and all of the maxes in bb, both arrays remain sorted.

    → Reply

      »
      »
      _Maddy1234
      9 months ago, show #

      9 months ago, hide # ^ |

      Top___Cat
      9 months ago, show #

      Top___Cat
      9 months ago, hide # ^ |
       
      0

      I solved the problem but couldn't understand why my solution actually works, thanks for explaining :)

      → Reply
  • »
    »
    countful
    9 months ago, show (+4) #
    »
    »
    countful
    9 months ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 2 →  
    +6

    After sorting, we have:
    - a[i] < a[i+1] for all i
    - b[i] < b[i+1] for all i

    Now, when we decide to swap because a[i] > b[i], we need to verify that the swap won't violate the sorted order of either array.

    Swapping b[i] into a:

    We're putting b[i] in place of a[i]. Since we originally had b[i] < a[i] < a[i+1], it follows that b[i] < a[i+1], so inserting b[i] into a won't break the increasing order in a.

    Swapping a[i] into b:

    This is more subtle. It's possible that a[i] > b[i+1], which would break the sorted order in b. However, since b[i+1] < a[i] < a[i+1], on the next iteration (i+1), we will again encounter a[i+1] > b[i+1], and thus perform another swap. This chain reaction will make sure that the arrays remain valid.

    → Reply
    • »

    inokamilyanin
    5 months ago, show #

    inokamilyanin
    5 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    simple proof for min(a[i], b[i]) < min(a[i + 1], b[i + 1])

    d1 = a[i + 1] - a[i], d1 > 0

    d2 = b[i + 1] - b[i], d2 > 0

    min(a[i], b[i]) < min(a[i] + d1, b[i] + d2) because both arguments of min have increased

    → Reply

sumit_kk10
9 months ago, show (+3) #
»
sumit_kk10
9 months ago, hide # |
← Rev. 3 →  
+2

Alternate solution for problem G:

Let dp[i] denote the sum of function f() over all substrings in prefix of i, i.e, ∑f(sl,sl+1,...,sr)∑f(sl,sl+1,...,sr)∀∀ l, r s.t. 1≤l≤r≤i1≤l≤r≤i. Let ans[i] denote the sum of function f() over all substrings ending at index i, i.e, ∑f(sl,sl+1,...,si)∑f(sl,sl+1,...,si)∀∀ l s.t. 1≤l≤i1≤l≤i. Let pre[i] denote the prefix sum till index i, i.e, pre[i]=∑ij=i(s[j]==1)pre[i]=∑j=ii(s[j]==1).

Final answer will be dp[n] and base case is dp[1] = 1 and ans[1] = 1 (assuming 1 based indexing).

Now suppose the ithith character is a 1 (small change in sign when si=0si=0), then:

dp[i]=dp[i−1]+ans[i−1]+∑ij=1(pre[i]−pre[j−1]>i−j+1−pre[i]+pre[j−1])dp[i]=dp[i−1]+ans[i−1]+∑j=1i(pre[i]−pre[j−1]>i−j+1−pre[i]+pre[j−1])

ans[i]=dp[i]−dp[i−1]ans[i]=dp[i]−dp[i−1]

The summation equation basically accounts for all the +1's we do when attach sisi to s1,2,...,i−1s1,2,...,i−1.

We can rearrange the summation equation and then use (ordered) set optimization to quickly compute the dp :)

→ Reply

    »
    subtomyonlycodes
    9 months ago, show (+1) #
    »

    subtomyonlycodes
    9 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    can you explain the ans[i-1] + part? i dont get it.

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      jobin491
      9 months ago, show #
      »
      »
      »
      jobin491
      9 months ago, hide # ^ |
      ← Rev. 2 →  
      +3

      suppose s[i]='1' then ans[i] stores the answer for all substrings ending at i now you can see that this value wont change a lot from ans[i-1] it only changes when adding the last element to a subset increases the majority and this increase is only by 1 so you just have to add this number of subsets to ans[i-1] to get ans[i].

      so find all such subsets using the difference in count of 0 and 1

      let pref[i] be count(1)-count(0) till i;

      then at all previous places where pref[j] < pref[i] form the required answer; ordered set does it quickly

      similarly you can derive it for when s[i]='0'

      → Reply

denk
9 months ago, show (+5) #
»
denk
9 months ago, hide # |
← Rev. 3 →  
+15

Problem G has solutions for O(n)

I a bit overkilled this task and wrote this O(n) solution that uses 2 stacks to recalculate the answer using information about the answer on the previous prefix. If you are interested in analyzing it, here is the code: 324911499.

But I found a more interesting and simpler for understanding solution that reaches O(n), written by Kilo_5723. This solution implements the same logic as the author's, but counts the sum of modules of the prefix with asymptotic O(n), instead of O(nlogn): 324868540

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    Soultaker
    9 months ago, show #
    »
    »
    Soultaker
    9 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +15

    That's a clever approach. Thanks for sharing it.

    I wanted to point out that the tutorial's solution can also be made O(n)O(n) trivially by using a counting sort instead of a comparison-based sort, since all the elements are between −n−n and nn.

    In fact, you can think of Kilo_5723's solution as doing an online counting sort: at the end of the loop, the array val contains a count of the elements of pref in the tutorial. The “online” part is that he updates the total for each character processed, which is why there are only additions/subtractions and no multiplications.

    → Reply
  • »
    »
    Pugavkin
    9 months ago, show (+3) #
    »
    »
    Pugavkin
    9 months ago, hide # ^ |

    Here's mine also with O(n) time

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      Haithom
      9 months ago, show (+2) #
      »
      »
      »
      Haithom
      9 months ago, hide # ^ |
       
      0

      Could you please explain how it works?

      → Reply
      • »
        »
        »
        »
        horribleCoder
        9 months ago, show (+1) #
        »
        »
        »
        »
        horribleCoder
        9 months ago, hide # ^ |
         
        +1

        Basically, when you have the answer to some prefix that is n characters long, to find the answer to prefix that is n+1 characters long(either adding a 1 or 0), all you need to know is what ranges have what ever character added as the (n+1)th as the most common character or have both 1 and 0 as the most common character. We can have an array of size n*2 +1 and have a middle index and move it up or down depending on if a character is 0 or 1, for instance down if 0, up if 1 as the most we can move up or down is n. In this case in the array whatever index we start on we can increment it, and keep track of how many increments (or just the sum) of the array on the left and right sides of the 'middle' index as for all the increments that where previously done on the right side, if the new character is 0, that new character is increasing the value of those sequences as those subsets had 0 most common character. The inverse is true for the left side. This is like dp where we are using our previous answer and adding onto it, with each new encounter during our iteration through the list.

        https://codeforces.com/contest/2121/submission/326062731

        → Reply

    9 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    I simply used a Lazy Segment Tree; after compressing all values, let dp[k][v]dp[k][v] be the maximum length of a non-decreasing subsequence using the first kk intervals and ending in vv.

    Initially (for k=0k=0), the maximum length is always 00. For a fixed kk, all non-decreasing subsequences ending in [0;l][0;l] can be "extended" by one by simply appending ll to it. At the same time, all subsequences ending in v∈[l;r]v∈[l;r] can be "extended" by vv; two operations that can be easily implemented for a Segment Tree!

    So in the end, you simply maintain a Lazy Segment Tree containing all possible ending values and update that for each step in O(logn)O(log⁡n) time.

    Submission: 324887791

    → Reply
»

  • »

    RishantS
    9 months ago, show (+3) #
    »
    »
    RishantS
    9 months ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 2 →  
    0

    The problem asks you to output the manimum possible maximum value. Meaning, you have to find out after doing one operation, what is the maximum value in the matrix? You have two cases, then

    1) Lets say the maximum number has a count of 1 in the entire matrix, thus, you can simply reduce it by 1 and that's your answer.

    2) If there are more than 1 count of the maximum in the matrix, then you will have to check if its possible to reduce all of them with one operation or not. If its possible, then the answer is mx — 1. If its not possible, then the answer is mx

    1 3 3 1 2 3 4 1 2 4 1 2

    In this test case, its possible to choose row = 2, and column 2 to reduce all the 4s to 3s. Thus, the final maximum value will be 3

    → Reply

    You're using inbuilt swap function which stores the elements it is swapping and since you're swapping around 1600 pairs worst case for every test case. for 100 test cases it will add and you'll have to store 160000 pairs which is causing the memory limit exceed. you can instead of using inbuilt swap function swap them manually and it should pass

I believe I have another approach for Problem D (324890440) where we construct the first row as:

1,2,3,…,N1,2,3,…,N

and the second row as:

N+1,N+2,N+3,…,2NN+1,N+2,N+3,…,2N.

Here's an analysis of the worst-case scenario for my solution:

Worst-case scenario input
1
40
79, 80, 77, 78, 75, 76, ..., 43, 44, 41, 42
40, 39, ..., 1

1. Fixing the first row

We need to sum the moves required for each element, which correspond to 40+38+36+…+2+2+4+6+…+4040+38+36+…+2+2+4+6+…+40

This sum is equal to 420+420=840420+420=840, so the total number of moves is: 20×21×2=84020×21×2=840

2. Fixing the second row

After the top row is sorted, the bottom row becomes:

2N,2N−1,...,N+12N,2N−1,...,N+1

This is a reverse-sorted sequence of size N=40N=40. Sorting it (e.g. via bubble sort or selection sort) in the worst case takes: 40×392=78040×392=780

Total moves: 840+780=1620840+780=1620 moves.

        #include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

#define ll long long
#define vi vector<int>
#define vll vector<long long>
#define vpii vector<pair<int,int>>
#define vpll vector<pair<long long,long long>>
#define pii pair<int,int>
#define pll pair<long long,long long>
#define mod1 1000000007
#define mod2 998244353
#define INF 1e18
#define pb push_back
#define ppb pop_back
#define mp make_pair
#define ff first
#define ss second
#define sz(x) ((ll)(x).size())
#define all(x) (x).begin(), (x).end()
#define rep(i,a,b) for(ll i=(a) ; i<(b) ; i++)
#define all(x) (x).begin(), (x).end()
#define yes cout<<"YES\n"
#define no cout<<"NO\n"
#define nl '\n'
#define read(a) for(auto &it : a) cin>>it;
#define printv(nums) for(int i=0;i<nums.size();i++) cout<<nums[i]<<" "; cout<<"\n";

#ifndef ONLINE_JUDGE
#include "template.cpp"
#else
#define debug(...)
#define debugArr(...)
#endif

void solve()
{
    int n;
    cin >> n;
    vi a(n),b(n);    
    read(a);
    read(b);
    vpii ans;
    for(int i=1;i<=2 * n;i++)
    {
        int idx = -1;
        char cur = 'a';
        for(int j=0;j<n;j++)
        {
            if(a[j] == i)
            {
                idx = j;
                cur = 'a';
                break;
            }
        }
        if(idx == -1)
        {
            for(int j=0;j<n;j++)
            {
                if(b[j] == i)
                {
                    idx = j;
                    cur = 'b';
                    break;
                }
            }   
        }
        int reqidx = (i <= n) ? i - 1 : i - n - 1;
        char reqA = (i <= n) ? 'a' : 'b';
        if(cur != reqA)
        {
            swap(a[idx],b[idx]);
            ans.pb({3,idx+1});
        }
        while(idx > reqidx)
        {
            if(cur == 'a') ans.pb({1,idx});
            else ans.pb({2,idx});
            if(cur == 'a') swap(a[idx] , a[idx-1]);
            else swap(b[idx] , b[idx-1]);
            idx--;
        }
        while(idx < reqidx)
        {
            if(cur == 'a') ans.pb({1,idx+1});
            else ans.pb({2,idx+1});
            if(cur == 'a') swap(a[idx] , a[idx+1]);
            else swap(b[idx] , b[idx+1]);
            idx++;
        }
    }
    cout << sz(ans) << nl;
    for(auto &[x,y] : ans) cout << x << " "<< y << nl;
}


signed main()
{
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false); cin.tie(NULL); cout.tie(NULL);
    freopen("Error.txt", "w", stderr);

    int t = 1;
    cin >> t;
    for (int i = 1; i <= t; i++)
    {
        solve();
    }

    return 0;
}

        On that tc:13 previously it was taking 1810 op but not 2160

Baby_Bird

0

Great contest and enjoyable problems!

A modified approach for Problem F. Observe that the array can be split into blocks by elements greater than xx (non-inclusive), i.e. a=[l1...r1,g1>x,l2...r2,g2>x,...,ls...rs]a=[l1...r1,g1>x,l2...r2,g2>x,...,ls...rs]. There will always be at least one block. Each block, likewise, can be split into sub-blocks (at least one) by elements that equal to xx (non-inclusive). If we count subarrays with the desired sum ss in a block, it will have all subarrays with a maximum of xx in that block and some subarrays which don't contain any maxima. These additional subarrays correspond to sub-blocks. So the answer is the sum of the count of subarrays with the desired sum ss across all blocks minus the sum of the count of subarrays with the desired sum ss across all sub-blocks in every block.

→ Reply
»

-8

I felt that the editorial for problem B was a bit too convoluted. I suggest the following solution:

OBSERVATION: 

1) String b, if it exists, will never constitute the first or last letters.

2) The minimum length of string b will be 1, and all possible string values b can take, if present, can be extended from the 1 you obtain, if present.

ALGORITHM:

Create a list containing the counts of all alphabets, and increment accordingly for each instance. Then from i=2 to i=n-1(in 1-based indexing), iterate through the string and see if any character in there has a count more than 2. If you find a character that has a count of more than 1, you can break and report YES. Else, after the iteration, report NO.

This is because as soon as you find such an alphabet, you can directly make that alphabet string b and the remaining string will have that character.

Please feel free to point out if I have missed anything.

→ Reply

sandbag

0

G can also be easily solved with a segment tree. First, get the sum of max counts for every substring containing the number at index 0. Keep track of the number of positive and negative relative one/zero counts for every substring. Then, we can remove the numbers from the left one by one. If we remove a 1, then decrease our result by the number of positive net sums and decrease all net sums by 1. If we remove a 0, then decrease our result by the number of negative net sums and increase all net sums by 1. Then the answer is the sum of each result after removing each number from the left n times.

Code: 326714584

→ Reply
»

0

Here is my O(n) solution for problem G.

    int n;
    cin>>n;
    string s;
    cin>>s;
    vector<stack<pair<int,int>>>delta_cnt_pairs(2);
    vector<int>exceed_cnt(2);
    int ans=0,sum=0,eq=0;

    for(auto i:s){
        int x = i-'0';
        int y = x^1;

        delta_cnt_pairs[x].push({1, eq + 1});
        exceed_cnt[x]+=eq + 1;
        eq=0;

        if(!delta_cnt_pairs[y].empty()){
            auto t=delta_cnt_pairs[y].top();
            delta_cnt_pairs[y].pop();
            if(t.first == 1){
                eq = t.second;
                exceed_cnt[y] -= eq;
            }
            else{
                delta_cnt_pairs[y].push({t.first - 1, t.second});
            }
        }
        sum+=exceed_cnt[x];
        ans+=sum;
    }
→ Reply

nipun_gupta31
8 months ago, show (+1) #

nipun_gupta31
8 months ago, hide # |
← Rev. 9 →  
0

proof for problem D solution -> (to ensure after sorting both a and b , if we do operation 3 on each index from 0 to n-1 => it doesn't disturb the sorting order of a and b)

we sort both a and b, now performing operation 3 on each index - let say first index where a[i] > b[i] is i = x => a[x] > b[x], then a[x-1] < b[x-1] => a[x-1] < b[x] (since, b[x-1] < b[x])

a[x + 1] > a[x] => a[x + 1] > b[x] so now if we swap a[x] and b[x] => a[x-1] < b[x] < a[x + 1] thus satisfying sorted ordering of a

in the same way b[x-1] < a[x] < b[x + 1] satisfying sorted ordering of b also

→ Reply

Simpler implementation for Problem E

Instead of checking the difference for each digit, we build each number from left to right, i.e. 1234512345 becomes 1,12,123,1234,123451,12,123,1234,12345 — we can do this by multiplying the current number by 1010 then adding the ii-th digit

Denote the answer ff. For each number of digits, if l=rl=r, f=f+2f=f+2; if l=r−1l=r−1, f=f+1f=f+1, else break

Time complexity is O(logr)O(log⁡r)

Python code: 368146164

This is my first time doing this so sorry if it's unclear
